questions.stem: A 65-year-old man, 3 days post-operative following open cholecystectomy, develops acute onset of painful swelling over the right parotid region. He is febrile (38.5°C) and has trismus. On examination, the right parotid is tender and swollen, and milking the duct produces purulent discharge. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Post-operative dehydration and purulent duct discharge point to bacterial sialadenitis (often due to S. aureus). Viral parotitis is typically bilateral and non-purulent; a stone would not cause fever or pus; abscess is a complication but not the primary diagnosis; lymphadenitis is not intra-glandular.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of pain and swelling in the left submandibular region, specifically triggered by eating. The swelling subsides gradually over an hour. Examination reveals a tender, firm left submandibular gland, and bimanual palpation of the floor of mouth reveals a hard lump in Wharton's duct. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Classic history of 'mealtime syndrome' with postprandial swelling and palpable duct stone confirms sialolithiasis. Sialadenitis would be more persistent and systemic; tumour is painless and not meal-related; lymph node is separate from gland; ranula is a cystic floor-of-mouth swelling.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of a slowly enlarging, painless lump in the left parotid region. She is otherwise well. On examination, there is a 3 cm firm, mobile, non-tender mass in the superficial lobe of the left parotid, with normal facial nerve function. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Slow-growing, painless, firm mobile mass in parotid superficial lobe is typical of pleomorphic adenoma (most common salivary tumour). Warthin's is often softer, cystic, and more common in smokers; carcinomas tend to be faster-growing or cause pain/nerve involvement; a lymph node would be softer and may be multiple.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old unvaccinated man presents with bilateral painful parotid swelling and low-grade fever. He has no purulent discharge from Stensen's ducts. He mentions that his 4-year-old nephew had mumps-like illness 2 weeks ago. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Bilateral parotitis with prodromal symptoms and exposure to mumps in an unvaccinated adult is classic for mumps. Bacterial sialadenitis is usually unilateral and purulent; Sjögren's is chronic and associated with xerostomia; stone causes unilateral meal-related pain; glandular fever can cause parotid swelling but often with lymphadenopathy and sore throat.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of a painless right parotid mass. On examination, there is a hard, fixed 4 cm mass in the parotid gland with associated weakness of the right lower lip (marginal mandibular branch of facial nerve). What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hard, fixed mass with facial nerve involvement is highly suspicious for malignant salivary gland tumour; mucoepidermoid carcinoma is the most common primary parotid malignancy. Pleomorphic adenoma and Warthin's are benign and do not cause nerve palsy; sialadenitis is painful and acute; lymphoma typically softer and may be bilateral.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with recurrent swelling of the right parotid gland, typically occurring just before meals. Between episodes she is asymptomatic. What is the single most appropriate first-line investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Ultrasound scan of the parotid gland
questions.explanation: Ultrasound is the first-line investigation for suspected sialolithiasis. It is sensitive for stones >2 mm and can assess gland texture. Plain film may miss radiolucent stones, while sialography is invasive and reserved for cases where ultrasound is equivocal.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with acute-onset painful swelling of the left submandibular gland, fever, and trismus. He has purulent discharge from Wharton's duct on massage. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis of acute bacterial sialadenitis?
options: Ultrasound scan of the submandibular gland
Swab of the duct orifice for culture
Sialography of the left submandibular gland
questions.explanation: Ultrasound is the first-line imaging for acute sialadenitis, showing gland enlargement, hypoechoic areas, and duct dilatation. CT is reserved for complications like abscess. Swab may guide antibiotics but is not diagnostic of the condition itself.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with known right parotid pleomorphic adenoma presents with new-onset ipsilateral facial nerve weakness and pain. What is the single most appropriate investigation to evaluate for malignant transformation?
options: CT scan of the neck and chest
questions.explanation: Contrast-enhanced MRI is the imaging modality of choice for assessing parotid tumors, especially when there is suspicion of malignancy (facial nerve involvement). It provides superior soft-tissue contrast to evaluate perineural spread and deep lobe extension.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with a 2-year history of dry eyes and dry mouth presents with bilateral, painless, firm parotid enlargement. Anti-Ro/La antibodies are negative. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm a diagnosis of Sjögren's syndrome?
questions.explanation: Labial salivary gland biopsy showing focal lymphocytic sialadenitis with a focus score ≥1 is a key diagnostic criterion for Sjögren's syndrome, especially in seronegative cases. Schirmer test and sialometry are supportive but not definitive, and ultrasound is ancillary.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man presents with a painful, fluctuant swelling over the left parotid region, with overlying erythema and high fever. He has trismus and difficulty opening his mouth. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the presence of a parotid abscess?
questions.explanation: CT neck with contrast is the gold standard for diagnosing a deep parotid abscess, as it clearly delineates fluid collections and assesses extension into deep neck spaces. Ultrasound may be limited by trismus and depth, while needle aspiration is therapeutic and diagnostic but not first-line imaging.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with acute onset of painful left parotid swelling, fever (38.2°C), and tenderness. There is no fluctuance. She is systemically well, nil allergies. Bloods show raised CRP. Diagnosis: acute bacterial sialadenitis. What is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends co-amoxiclav as first-line for acute bacterial sialadenitis due to its cover of Staph aureus and oral anaerobes. Flucloxacillin is a common alternative but does not cover anaerobes; clarithromycin is used in penicillin allergy.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man has recurrent painful swelling of the right submandibular gland during meals. Examination is normal between meals. CT shows a 4 mm stone in Wharton's duct with mild ductal dilatation. Diagnosis: sialolithiasis. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Conservative management with hydration and sialogogues
questions.explanation: Stones <5 mm often pass spontaneously; conservative management with sialogogues (e.g., sour candies) and hydration increases saliva flow, aiding stone expulsion. Invasive interventions are reserved for persistent symptoms or larger stones.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with poorly controlled diabetes presents with acute painful swelling of the left parotid, high fever (39.5°C), trismus, and a fluctuant mass. CT shows a parotid abscess. Diagnosis: parotid abscess secondary to sialadenitis. What is the definitive treatment?
options: Incision and drainage of the abscess
questions.explanation: An abscess requires surgical drainage; incision and drainage is definitive. Antibiotics alone are insufficient. Needle aspiration may be used for small collections but I&D is standard for large abscesses.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman has a 2-year history of a slow-growing, painless mass in the left parotid. Fine-needle aspiration shows pleomorphic adenoma. Diagnosis: pleomorphic adenoma. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Superficial parotidectomy with facial nerve preservation
questions.explanation: The standard treatment for pleomorphic adenoma in the superficial lobe is conservative superficial parotidectomy, which preserves the facial nerve. Enucleation is contraindicated due to high recurrence risk from pseudopodia.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with recurrent sialadenitis presents with an acute exacerbation. She is currently taking hyoscine butylbromide for irritable bowel syndrome. Which medication is contraindicated in acute sialadenitis?
questions.explanation: Hyoscine butylbromide is an anticholinergic that suppresses salivary secretion, worsening stasis and promoting infection. All other options are safe or indicated: co-amoxiclav is first-line antibiotic, NSAIDs and paracetamol provide analgesia, prochlorperazine may be used for nausea.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man, day 3 post laparotomy for bowel obstruction, develops acute right parotid swelling, erythema, and pain. Temperature 38.5°C, WCC 18, CRP 150. He is on IV fluids but has been nil by mouth. What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Take a swab from Stensen's duct
questions.explanation: This is acute bacterial sialadenitis in a dehydrated, post-op patient; priority is IV antibiotics to cover oral flora (e.g. co-amoxiclav). Ultrasound may be done but not before antibiotics.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents to ED with a 4-hour history of acute painful swelling of the left submandibular region, which occurs only during meals. She is otherwise well, afebrile. On examination, tender swelling over left submandibular gland, no pus at Wharton's duct. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange urgent ultrasound of salivary glands
questions.explanation: The history is classic for an obstructing stone; first-line imaging for suspected sialolithiasis is ultrasound (NICE). Antibiotics are not indicated without infection.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man is referred to ENT clinic with a 2-year history of a slow-growing, painless lump in the left parotid region. Examination reveals a 3cm firm, mobile mass in the superficial lobe. FNAC shows pleomorphic adenoma. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: Pleomorphic adenoma is benign but has risk of malignant transformation; definitive treatment is surgical excision (superficial parotidectomy). Imaging may be done preoperatively but referral is the next step after diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old diabetic woman presents with a 3-day history of worsening right parotid swelling, pain, and fever. On examination, she is unwell, temperature 39°C, the gland is erythematous, and there is a fluctuant area. What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Aspirate the fluctuant area for culture
questions.explanation: A fluctuant abscess requires surgical drainage (I&D) as the priority; antibiotics alone are insufficient. Ultrasound may confirm but should not delay drainage.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman underwent superficial parotidectomy for a benign tumour 1 week ago. She now presents with a soft swelling over the operative site, no erythema, no fever. Aspiration yields clear saliva. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange ultrasound to assess for collection
questions.explanation: This is a sialocele (salivary collection) post-parotidectomy. First-line is aspiration and application of a pressure dressing to prevent reaccumulation. Antibiotics are not indicated.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with acute painful swelling of the left parotid region for 2 days, with erythema and tenderness. He is febrile (38.5°C) and has trismus. Which finding indicates the need for urgent surgical review?
questions.explanation: Trismus suggests involvement of the masticator or parapharyngeal space, a red flag for deep neck infection requiring urgent ENT assessment. Fever and erythema are common in sialadenitis but not immediate surgical thresholds.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with a slow-growing, painless lump in the right parotid for 6 months. On examination, there is a firm, mobile nodule in the superficial lobe. Which finding would raise concern for malignancy?
questions.explanation: Facial nerve weakness indicates perineural invasion, a hallmark of malignant parotid tumours. Benign lesions rarely cause facial palsy; absence of nerve involvement is typical.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with recurrent submandibular sialadenitis due to a stone reports acute worsening of pain and swelling after a meal. On examination, the gland is tender and swollen, but there is no pus. Which clinical feature suggests imminent abscess formation?
questions.explanation: Fluctuance indicates a collection of pus requiring drainage. Pain with eating is typical of sialolithiasis; stone palpation confirms the cause but does not signal abscess.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman, 3 days post-hip replacement, develops acute swelling of the left submandibular region, with pain and difficulty swallowing. She is dehydrated. Which finding is most concerning for airway compromise?
options: Swelling of the floor of mouth
questions.explanation: Floor of mouth swelling can elevate the tongue and obstruct the airway, heralding Ludwig's angina or deep neck infection. Systemic inflammatory markers are important but not directly airway-threatening.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known Sjögren's syndrome complains of a rapidly enlarging, firm mass in the right parotid over 3 weeks. He has no fever. Which diagnosis must be excluded?
questions.explanation: Sjögren's syndrome carries a 16-fold increased risk of non-Hodgkin lymphoma, which can present as a rapidly enlarging salivary mass. Acute infection or stones are less likely without fever or colicky pain.
